title: Calligraphy on dark paper with white gouache
subtitle: Reversing the ground, white and metallic writing on black and deep-coloured stock
excerpt: Writing on dark paper with white or metallic gouache reverses the conventional relationship between letter and ground. The technique requires a slightly stiffer consistency than ink-on-white work, a clean nib, and enough gouache coverage that the dark paper does not show through in a single pass. Used in manuscript illumination traditions for decorative titles and display lines on black or deep-coloured vellum and paper.
sourceNotes: Edward Johnston, Writing and Illuminating and Lettering (1906), Johnston discusses writing on dark grounds with white and gold, including the use of white gouache and shell gold for title pages and display work. Internet Archive: https://archive.org/details/writingilluminat00john.

glossaryTerms.definition: Powdered gold bound with gum arabic, supplied in a small dish or shell. Applied with a fine brush, it produces a matte metallic writing surface. A period-accurate alternative to gold leaf for small lettering; less brilliant than burnished leaf but easier to apply with a pen or brush.

An opaque paint medium, historically, white lead or chalk-based pigment bound with gum, applied with a pen or brush to write on dark grounds. Modern calligraphy gouache is the contemporary equivalent.

The ability of gouache to obscure the paper beneath it in a single pass. Coverage depends on pigment load, consistency, and the opacity of the specific pigment. Titanium white has high coverage; many colours do not.

content.text: on dark paper has been used since the twelfth century. White or gold letters on deep blue, black, or crimson pages appear throughout illuminated Books of Hours. The technique is not hard, but any uneven stroke shows against the dark ground, and too-thin gouache leaves a semi-transparent mark that exposes the paper beneath.

items.name: Calligraphy gouache, titanium white (or Permanent White)
items.substitutions: Chinese white watercolour, slightly less opaque; Zinc white, do not use on dark grounds (too transparent)

items.name: Black cartridge paper, 220 gsm
items.substitutions: Deep-coloured endpaper stock in navy, burgundy, or bottle green

content.text: Mix the gouache slightly thicker than for white-paper work, closer to double cream rather than single cream. The extra body is needed to achieve full

in one pass. Test every batch before writing: stroke a small sample on the dark paper, allow it to dry, and hold it at an angle to the light. Any hint of the dark paper showing through means the mix is too thin.
Rest your writing hand on the tracing-paper guard throughout, do not touch the dark paper with bare fingers. Dark paper picks up oils and scuffs more visibly than white.
Load the nib from the underside as with any gouache work. Stroke onto scrap dark paper first; the first stroke after loading nearly always gives a slightly thin line as excess ink is deposited.
Write slowly. On dark paper the slower stroke deposits more gouache and improves coverage. Fast strokes skim over the surface with less contact and can leave a thin, see-through line.

Shell gold is gum-arabic-bound gold powder applied with a pen or fine brush. Mix the shell gold with a small amount of distilled water and load onto the nib as for gouache. Shell gold on a black ground gives a warm, matte gold letter, less brilliant than burnished leaf but achievable with a pen. It dries quickly; reload every letter.

items.symptom: White letters look grey rather than bright white
items.cause: Too much water in the mix; insufficient pigment load.
items.fix: Thicken the mix by allowing water to evaporate from the palette for a minute, or add a little fresh gouache from the tube. Titanium white should dry a clean bright white.

items.symptom: Gouache cracks off the dark paper after drying
items.cause: Too thick a film applied in one stroke; the film shrinks on drying.
items.fix: Thin the mix slightly and add a drop of gum arabic to increase adhesion.
